Title: Working With Claude Code: A Field Manual
Description:
Last week I published a reflective post on how agentic coding has changed my working day and the shape of the profession. I now want to turn to the other side of that coin: not the philosophy, but the mechanics — the habits, the workflows, the accumulated tricks that turn agentic coding from an occasionally impressive demo into a dependable way of working.
This post is my field manual. It’s what I’ve learned over the past few months, mostly by getting things wrong and slowly figuring out better approaches. Your mileage will vary, but all of this is what I actually do, now, on a working Thursday in April 2026.

Title: Mind the van Emden Gap
Description:
I recently read an interesting paper written in 1982 by M.H. van
Emden titled “Mental Ergonomics as Basis for New-Generation Computer
Systems.” In the paper, van Emden described a “conversational partner”
as a way to deal with the problem of software complexity at the time. He
was also looking for a “congenial tool” for thought-workers that he
called “Computer-Aided Thought” (CAT). He viewed his ideal
conversational partner as a projection of his own reasoning that could
react, critique, and extend his ideas as an interlocutor. He also saw
the ideal CAT interlocutor as one that provided a source of productive
friction by pushing back on his ideas to help stimulate more careful
thinking. He didn’t expect that this conversational partner to have any
true knowledge, but instead valued the reflexive nature of the tool in
response to the valuable act of deliberately structuring his thoughts in
such a way as to stimulate the tool. The process of structuring his
thoughts in such a way to provoke the tool took a systematic form where
each idea was broken into smaller sub-thoughts (so to speak), each
precisely stated and each following from the last. Once he fed this into
the CAT system, he hoped to observe how the system responded (or not) to
his “argument” (for lack of a better term). When the system pushed-back
he could revise the structure and contents of his argument to drive
towards deeper understanding. He thought that these arguments should be
written in a formal representation that enforced explicitness and
logical support.1 He preferred a kind of logical
language over English2 because it was more formal and
necessarily constrained in ways that English isn’t. He saw the fluidity
and ambiguity of English as a circumvention of the very process of
formalizing one’s thoughts, something that van Emden viewed as critical
to intellectual growth, and thus the necessary driving force behind how
CAT systems should have been built.

Title: On sabotaging projects by overthinking, scope creep, and structural diffing
Why I Cancelled Claude: Token Issues, Declining Quality, and Poor Support
Description:
First enthusiasm A couple of weeks ago I subscribed to Claude Code, and during the first few weeks I had a really nice experience. It was fast, the token allowance was fair, and the quality was good.
I learned they had raised the token allowance for non-rush hours , and since they opposed some governmental rules, it felt good to support the right cause.
(づ ￣ ³￣)づ
However… for about three weeks now my initial enthusiasm has been rapidly waning.

Title: Husband and Wife
New 10 GbE USB adapters are cooler, smaller, cheaper
Description:
For years, the best way to get 10 gigabit networking on laptops was to buy an expensive, large, and hot 10 GbE Thunderbolt adapter. With new RTL8159-based 10G USB 3.2 adapters coming onto the market, the bulky adapters might be a thing of the past. Just look at the size of the thing in comparison to my Thunderbolt adapters:
2.5G and even 5G USB adapters have been out for a while, but sometimes you need more bandwidth.

Title: Software engineering may no longer be a lifetime career
Description: I don’t think there’s compelling evidence that using AI makes you less intelligent overall1. However, it seems pretty obvious that using AI to perform a task means you don’t learn as much about performing that task. Some software engineers think this is a decisive argument against the use of AI. Their argument goes something like this:
